text: During thoracoscopy, the surgeon identifies stiff adhesions between the involved left lower lobe, diaphragm, and mediastinum.
The surgeon uses a high-energy device to dissect adhesions, achieving effective hemostasis during the procedure.
The surgeon approaches the fissure after freeing the lung parenchyma, dissecting the branches of the pulmonary artery supplying the lower lobe.
Enlarged lymph nodes are visibly surrounding the vessels in the surgical field.
The surgeon observes a markedly enlarged bronchial artery located in the posterior aspect of the pulmonary hilum, positioned just behind the apical segmental artery.
The surgeon uses a stapling device to suture the vessel after completing the section, ensuring secure closure of the vessel.
The surgeon identifies a pathological bronchial artery located within the pulmonary ligament, visible amidst the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon isolates and closes the lower pulmonary vein using a high-energy device, ensuring safe ligation of the vessel.
The surgeon completes the fissure and closes the bronchus, with visible manipulation of the bronchial structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon extracts the specimen using an endo bag, ensuring containment and safe removal from the surgical field.
The surgeon inserts a single chest tube through the posterior axis at the end of the thymectomy procedure.
